content:
# Disagreement Log — Numbers 16 (NUM_016)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA


### Verse 1 — The Object of "took" (וַיִּקַּח)
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "And-took Korah son of Izhar..." (no object supplied; bare verb preserved with interlinear gloss) |
| **LOGOS** | "And Korah son of Izhar...took —" (em-dash, no object supplied; elliptical) |
| **PNEUMA** | "And Korah son of Izhar...took men —" (supplies "men" as implicit object) |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I follow LOGOS — retain the MT's elliptical construction with an em-dash after "took," preserving the grammatical anomaly of the Leningrad Codex. The LXX (Vaticanus) smooths with *kai elalesen* ("and he spoke"), but the MT's abruptness is theologically and narratively significant. PNEUMA's "took men" is defensible but adds what the Hebrew deliberately omits.

---

### Verse 2 — "leaders of the congregation" vs. "leaders of the community"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "leaders of the-congregation, called-ones of the-assembly, men of name" |
| **LOGOS** | "leaders of the congregation, those called to the assembly, men of renown" |
| **PNEUMA** | "leaders of the community, representatives chosen by the assembly, men of renown" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt LOGOS. Hebrew *nesi'ei ha-edah* is "leaders of the congregation" (not "community"). PNEUMA's "representatives chosen by the assembly" over-interprets *qeri'ei mo'ed* ("called ones of the assembly/meeting"). "Men of renown" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) better conveys *anshei shem* than EMET's raw "men of name."

---

### Verse 3 — "exalt yourselves" vs. "elevate yourselves" vs. "lift yourselves up"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "why do-you-lift-yourselves-up over the-assembly of YHWH?" |
| **LOGOS** | "Why then do you exalt yourselves above the assembly of YHWH?" |
| **PNEUMA** | "So why do you elevate yourselves above the assembly of YHWH?" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt "exalt yourselves" (LOGOS). The Hebrew *titnasse'u* (from *nasa'*, to lift) carries a sense of presumptuous self-elevation; "exalt" captures the theological weight. PNEUMA's "elevate" is close but lacks the sacral overtone. EMET's hyphenated literalism is preserved in interlinear but not in consensus reading text.

---

### Verse 5 — "his congregation" vs. "his followers"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "all his-congregation" |
| **LOGOS** | "all his congregation" |
| **PNEUMA** | "all his followers" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt "congregation" (EMET/LOGOS). The Hebrew *adato* means "his congregation/assembly." PNEUMA's "followers" is interpretive and loses the formal assembly language that characterizes this chapter.

---

### Verse 5 — "bring near" vs. "draw near"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "he-will-bring-near unto-him" |
| **LOGOS** | "he will bring him near to himself" |
| **PNEUMA** | "he will draw that one near to himself" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt "bring near" (EMET/LOGOS). Hebrew *ve-hiqriv elav* uses the Hiphil of *qarav* — causative "bring near," not the self-directed "draw near." PNEUMA's "draw" subtly shifts the agency.

---

### Verse 6 — "censers" vs. "fire-pans"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "fire-pans" (*makhtot*) |
| **LOGOS** | "censers" |
| **PNEUMA** | "fire-pans" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt "censers" (LOGOS). While *makhtot* literally means "fire-pans" (from *khatah*, to snatch fire), the English term "censers" is the established liturgical translation for incense-bearing vessels. This is a case where formal equivalence serves clarity; both EMET and PNEUMA's "fire-pans" is more literal but less recognizable. The consensus uses "censers" throughout.

---

### Verse 7 — "it shall be" clause structure
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "and-it-shall-be, the-man whom YHWH will-choose — he is the-holy-one" |
| **LOGOS** | "and it shall be that the man whom YHWH chooses, he is the holy one" |
| **PNEUMA** | "And the man whom YHWH chooses -- he is the holy one" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt LOGOS. The Hebrew *ve-hayah* ("and it shall be") is present in the MT and should be rendered. PNEUMA omits it. LOGOS preserves the construction naturally.

---

### Verse 8 — "Hear now" vs. "Hear, please" vs. "Listen now"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "Hear, please" (*shim'u na*) |
| **LOGOS** | "Hear now" |
| **PNEUMA** | "Listen now" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt "Hear now" (LOGOS). Hebrew *shim'u na* — the particle *na* softens the imperative. "Hear now" preserves the biblical register. "Listen now" (PNEUMA) is acceptable but slightly informal. "Hear, please" (EMET) captures *na* as politeness but reads awkwardly in English.

---

### Verse 9 — "separated" vs. "set apart" / "congregation" vs. "community"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "has-separated...from the-congregation of Israel" |
| **LOGOS** | "has separated you from the congregation of Israel" |
| **PNEUMA** | "has set you apart from the community of Israel" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt "separated...from the congregation" (EMET/LOGOS). Hebrew *hivdil* is "separated" (standard). PNEUMA's "set apart" is interpretive and "community" departs from *adat* (congregation).

---

### Verse 9 — "Tabernacle" vs. "tabernacle"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "the-Tabernacle of YHWH" (capitalized) |
| **LOGOS** | "the Tabernacle of YHWH" (capitalized) |
| **PNEUMA** | "the tabernacle of YHWH" (lowercase) |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt capitalized "Tabernacle" (EMET/LOGOS). The Hebrew *mishkan YHWH* refers to the specific dwelling-place of YHWH; capitalization is warranted as a proper designation.

---

### Verse 10 — "and yet you seek" vs. "and now you seek"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "and-you-seek also priesthood" |
| **LOGOS** | "and yet you seek the priesthood also" |
| **PNEUMA** | "and now you seek the priesthood as well" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt LOGOS — "and yet you seek the priesthood also." The Hebrew *u-viqqashtem gam kehunnah* uses *gam* ("also/even"), which "yet...also" captures as both adversative and additive. PNEUMA's "now...as well" weakens the rhetorical force.

---

### Verse 11 — "who have gathered together" vs. "the-ones-assembled" / "your congregation" vs. "your followers"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "you and-all your-congregation, the-ones-assembled against YHWH" |
| **LOGOS** | "you and all your congregation who have gathered together are against YHWH" |
| **PNEUMA** | "it is against YHWH that you and all your followers have gathered together" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt LOGOS for word order and vocabulary. "Congregation" over "followers" (see v. 5 ruling). LOGOS preserves the Hebrew clause structure while remaining readable.

---

### Verse 12 — "sent to call" vs. "sent word to summon"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "And-sent Moses to-call for-Dathan and-for-Abiram" |
| **LOGOS** | "And Moses sent to call Dathan and Abiram" |
| **PNEUMA** | "And Moses sent word to summon Dathan and Abiram" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt LOGOS. Hebrew *va-yishlakh...liqro* is literally "sent to call." PNEUMA's "sent word to summon" adds "word" (not in Hebrew) and upgrades "call" to "summon."

---

### Verse 13 — Infinitive absolute rendering (כִּי תִשְׂתָּרֵר עָלֵינוּ גַּם־הִשְׂתָּרֵר)
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "you-lord-over us — lording, you-lord" (raw doubling) |
| **LOGOS** | "make yourself a prince over us — over and over again" |
| **PNEUMA** | "lord it over us, making yourself a ruler" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** This is a critical verse for the binding convention on infinitive absolute with doubling. The Hebrew *ki tishtarer aleinu gam histarer* uses the Hitpael of *sarar* (to rule/be a prince) with the infinitive absolute intensifier. Per convention, the doubling must be preserved. EMET's raw interlinear is too opaque. LOGOS paraphrases the doubling as "over and over again," which captures repetition but loses the verbal root. PNEUMA splits the idea into two clauses but does not formally double. I render: "make yourself a ruler over us — ruling, you would rule" — preserving the infinitive absolute doubling convention while using "ruler" (from *sarar*) rather than "prince."

---

### Verse 14 — "Will you gouge out" vs. "Would you gouge out"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "The-eyes of those men will-you-gouge-out?" |
| **LOGOS** | "Will you gouge out the eyes of these men?" |
| **PNEUMA** | "Would you gouge out the eyes of these men?" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt "Will you gouge out" (EMET/LOGOS). The Hebrew *ha-einei...tenaqer* uses the imperfect with interrogative *he*, which is a direct challenge ("will you?"), not a subjunctive ("would you?").

---

### Verse 15 — "Do not regard" vs. "Do not turn to" vs. "Do not pay any regard to"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "Do-not turn unto their-offering" |
| **LOGOS** | "Do not regard their offering" |
| **PNEUMA** | "Do not pay any regard to their offering" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt "Do not regard" (LOGOS). Hebrew *al tefen el minkhatam* — *panah el* means "turn to / regard." "Regard" captures the meaning cleanly. PNEUMA's "pay any regard to" adds emphasis not in the Hebrew. EMET's "turn unto" is literal but archaic.

---

### Verse 15 — "done harm to" vs. "done evil to" vs. "wronged"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "not have-I-done-evil to one of-them" |
| **LOGOS** | "nor have I done harm to any one of them" |
| **PNEUMA** | "I have not wronged a single one of them" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt "done harm" (LOGOS). Hebrew *hare'oti* (Hiphil of *ra'a*) means "caused evil/harm." "Done harm" is faithful without the theological loading of "done evil" or the interpretive compression of "wronged."

---

### Verse 16 — "be present before YHWH" vs. "present yourselves before YHWH"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "be before YHWH" |
| **LOGOS** | "be present before YHWH" |
| **PNEUMA** | "present yourselves before YHWH" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt "be present before YHWH" (LOGOS). Hebrew *heyu lifnei YHWH* is literally "be before YHWH." LOGOS adds "present" for English clarity without changing meaning. PNEUMA's reflexive "present yourselves" adds a nuance not explicit in the Hebrew.

---

### Verse 17 — "put incense upon them" vs. "place incense on it/them"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "and-you-shall-put upon-them incense" |
| **LOGOS** | "and put incense upon them" |
| **PNEUMA** | "and place incense on it" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt LOGOS. The Hebrew has plural *aleihem* ("upon them"), referring to the censers. PNEUMA's singular "on it" departs from the Hebrew.

---

### Verse 21 — "in a moment" vs. "in an instant"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "in-a-moment" |
| **LOGOS** | "in a moment" |
| **PNEUMA** | "in an instant" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt "in a moment" (EMET/LOGOS). Hebrew *ke-rega* means "like a moment/instant." Both are valid; majority rules, and "moment" is the more traditional rendering.

---

### Verse 22 — "be angry with" vs. "be wrathful" vs. "pour out your wrath on"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "and-upon all the-congregation will-you-be-wrathful?" |
| **LOGOS** | "shall one man sin and you be angry with the entire congregation?" |
| **PNEUMA** | "shall one man sin and you pour out your wrath on the entire community?" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt "be angry with" (LOGOS). Hebrew *tiqtsof* (from *qatsaf*, to be angry/wrathful) is a simple verb. "Be angry" is the unmarked translation. PNEUMA's "pour out your wrath" amplifies beyond the Hebrew verb. EMET's "be wrathful" is accurate but heavy.

---

### Verse 24 — "Get up from around" vs. "Move away from all around"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "Go-up from-around the-dwelling of Korah, Dathan, and-Abiram" |
| **LOGOS** | "Get up from around the dwelling of Korah, Dathan, and Abiram" |
| **PNEUMA** | "Move away from all around the dwelling of Korah, Dathan, and Abiram" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt LOGOS. Hebrew *he'alu mi-saviv le-mishkan* is literally "go up from around the dwelling." "Get up from around" captures both the upward motion (*he'alu*) and the surrounding position (*mi-saviv*). PNEUMA's "move away" loses the vertical aspect.

---

### Verse 25 — "went after him" vs. "followed him"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "and-went after-him the-elders of Israel" |
| **LOGOS** | "and the elders of Israel went after him" |
| **PNEUMA** | "and the elders of Israel followed him" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt "went after him" (EMET/LOGOS). Hebrew *va-yelkhu akharav* is literally "and they went after him." PNEUMA's "followed" is interpretive shorthand.

---

### Verse 26 — "Turn aside now" vs. "Turn away, please"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "Turn-aside, please, from-upon the-tents of the-men the-wicked" |
| **LOGOS** | "Turn aside now from the tents of these wicked men" |
| **PNEUMA** | "Turn away, please, from the tents of these wicked men" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt LOGOS. Hebrew *suru na me-al ohalei* — *suru* is "turn aside/depart," *na* is the softening particle. "Turn aside now" (LOGOS) is idiomatic and preserves register. "Turn away" (PNEUMA) is acceptable but "aside" better matches *sur*.

---

### Verse 27 — "little ones" vs. "small children"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "their-little-ones" (*tappam*) |
| **LOGOS** | "their little ones" |
| **PNEUMA** | "their small children" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt "little ones" (EMET/LOGOS). Hebrew *tappam* is a standard term for young children/toddlers. "Little ones" is the traditional English rendering.

---

### Verse 28 — "works" vs. "deeds"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "all the-deeds the-these" (*ha-ma'asim*) |
| **LOGOS** | "all these works" |
| **PNEUMA** | "all these deeds" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt "deeds" (EMET/PNEUMA). Hebrew *ha-ma'asim* can be "works" or "deeds." Both are valid, but "deeds" has a slight majority and fits the context of divinely commissioned actions.

---

### Verse 29 — "death of all mankind" vs. "common death of all humanity"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "like-the-death of all men these will-die" |
| **LOGOS** | "If these men die the death of all mankind" |
| **PNEUMA** | "If these men die the common death of all humanity" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt LOGOS. Hebrew *ke-mot kol ha-adam* is "like the death of all mankind." PNEUMA adds "common" (not in Hebrew). LOGOS is faithful and clear.

---

### Verse 30 — "creates something new" vs. "creates a creation" vs. "creates something utterly new"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "a-creation YHWH will-create" (cognate accusative) |
| **LOGOS** | "if YHWH creates something new" |
| **PNEUMA** | "if YHWH creates something utterly new" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** The Hebrew *beriah yivra YHWH* is a cognate accusative: "a creation YHWH creates." Per the binding convention on infinitive absolute with doubling, the cognate construction must be preserved. I render "if YHWH creates a creation" — retaining the Hebrew's doubling of the root *bara'*. LOGOS smooths to "something new" and PNEUMA amplifies to "utterly new"; both lose the cognate force. EMET's raw rendering is closest; I normalize it for English while preserving the doubling.

---

### Verse 30 — "despised YHWH" vs. "treated YHWH with contempt"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "have-despised...YHWH" |
| **LOGOS** | "have despised YHWH" |
| **PNEUMA** | "have treated YHWH with contempt" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt "have despised YHWH" (EMET/LOGOS). Hebrew *ni'atsu...YHWH* (from *na'ats*, to despise/spurn) is a single verb. PNEUMA's periphrastic "treated with contempt" dilutes the directness.

---

### Verse 31 — "And it came to pass" vs. "And the moment"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "And-it-was as-his-finishing to-speak all the-words" |
| **LOGOS** | "And it came to pass, as he finished speaking all these words" |
| **PNEUMA** | "And the moment he finished speaking all these words" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt LOGOS. Hebrew *va-yehi ke-khalloto le-dabber* uses the *va-yehi* narrative formula ("and it came to pass"), which should be preserved as a structural marker. PNEUMA's "the moment" is dynamic but loses the formulaic Hebrew narrative construction.

---

### Verse 32 — "households" vs. "houses" / "people" vs. "men"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "their-houses...all the-men who belonged-to-Korah...all the-property" |
| **LOGOS** | "their households...all the people who belonged to Korah...all their possessions" |
| **PNEUMA** | "their households...every person who belonged to Korah...all their possessions" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt LOGOS. Hebrew *batteihem* can mean "houses" or "households" — here "households" captures the sense of entire family units being swallowed. "All the people" (LOGOS) for *kol ha-adam* is better than EMET's "men" (which could exclude women/children) or PNEUMA's "every person" (which individualizes what Hebrew treats collectively). "Possessions" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) for *ha-rekhush* is better than EMET's "property."

---

### Verse 34 — "at their cry" vs. "at the sound of their cries"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "at-their-voice" |
| **LOGOS** | "at their cry" |
| **PNEUMA** | "at the sound of their cries" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt "at their cry" (LOGOS). Hebrew *le-qolam* is "at their voice/sound." LOGOS captures this concisely. PNEUMA elaborates beyond the Hebrew. EMET's "voice" misses that the context demands the sound of screaming.

---

### Verse 34 — "Lest the earth swallow us" vs. "The earth might swallow us too"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "Lest the-earth swallow-us!" |
| **LOGOS** | "Lest the earth swallow us!" |
| **PNEUMA** | "The earth might swallow us too!" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt "Lest the earth swallow us!" (EMET/LOGOS). Hebrew *pen tivlaenu ha-arets* uses *pen* ("lest"), which should be preserved. PNEUMA converts to a declarative statement.

---

### Verse 37 — "from the midst of the burning" vs. "from the blaze"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "from-between the-burning" |
| **LOGOS** | "from the midst of the burning" |
| **PNEUMA** | "from the blaze" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt LOGOS. Hebrew *mi-bein ha-serephah* is "from between/midst of the burning." PNEUMA's "blaze" is overly dynamic. EMET's "from-between" is literal but "midst of" is smoother English for *mi-bein* in this context.

---

### Verse 38 — "sinners" vs. "men who sinned" / "against their souls" vs. "at the cost of their lives"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "fire-pans of the-sinners the-these against-their-souls" |
| **LOGOS** | "the censers of these sinners at the cost of their lives" |
| **PNEUMA** | "the fire-pans of these men who sinned at the cost of their own lives" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt LOGOS. "Sinners" for *ha-khatta'im* is more concise than PNEUMA's periphrastic "men who sinned." "At the cost of their lives" well renders *be-nafshotehem*. See v. 6 ruling for "censers" over "fire-pans."

---

### Verse 39 — "which those who were burned had presented" vs. "that those who had been burned had brought"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "which brought-near the-ones-who-were-burned" |
| **LOGOS** | "which those who were burned had presented" |
| **PNEUMA** | "that those who had been burned had brought" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt LOGOS. "Presented" for *hiqrivu* (Hiphil of *qarav*) captures the cultic act of bringing an offering near. PNEUMA's "brought" is too generic.

---

### Verse 40 — "his congregation" vs. "his followers"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "like-Korah and-like-his-congregation" |
| **LOGOS** | "like Korah and his congregation" |
| **PNEUMA** | "like Korah and his followers" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt "congregation" (EMET/LOGOS), consistent with rulings throughout. Hebrew *adato*.

---

### Verse 41 — "on the next day" vs. "the very next day"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "on-the-morrow" |
| **LOGOS** | "on the next day" |
| **PNEUMA** | "the very next day" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt "on the next day" (LOGOS). Hebrew *mi-makhorat* means "on the morrow/next day." PNEUMA's "the very next day" adds emphasis ("very") not in the Hebrew. EMET's "on the morrow" is archaic.

---

### Verse 42 — "behold" vs. "there"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "and-behold, covered-it the-cloud" |
| **LOGOS** | "and behold, the cloud covered it" |
| **PNEUMA** | "and there the cloud had covered it" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt LOGOS. Hebrew *ve-hinneh kissahu he-anan* uses *hinneh* ("behold"), a presentative particle that should be preserved. PNEUMA's "there" loses the surprise/immediacy of *hinneh*.

---

### Verse 45 — "in a moment" vs. "in an instant"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "in-a-moment" |
| **LOGOS** | "in a moment" |
| **PNEUMA** | "in an instant" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt "in a moment" (EMET/LOGOS), consistent with v. 21 ruling. Hebrew *ke-rega*.

---

### Verse 46 — "the censer" vs. "the fire-pan" / "wrath has gone out from before YHWH" vs. "the wrath has gone out from YHWH"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "Take the fire-pan...for has-gone-out the-wrath from-before YHWH" |
| **LOGOS** | "Take the censer...for wrath has gone out from before YHWH" |
| **PNEUMA** | "Take the fire-pan...for the wrath has gone out from YHWH" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt LOGOS. "Censer" per v. 6 ruling. "From before YHWH" (*mi-lifnei YHWH*) preserves the spatial theological language of the Hebrew — wrath proceeds from the divine presence. PNEUMA's "from YHWH" drops *lifnei* ("before the face of").

---

### Verse 47 — "as Moses had spoken" vs. "just as Moses had spoken"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "as-which spoke Moses" |
| **LOGOS** | "as Moses had spoken" |
| **PNEUMA** | "just as Moses had spoken" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt LOGOS. Hebrew *ka-asher dibber Mosheh* is "as Moses spoke/had spoken." PNEUMA's "just as" adds slight emphasis. LOGOS is clean and faithful.

---

### Verse 48 — "was checked" vs. "was restrained" vs. "was stopped"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "was-restrained the-plague" |
| **LOGOS** | "the plague was checked" |
| **PNEUMA** | "the plague was stopped" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt "was checked" (LOGOS). Hebrew *va-te'atsar ha-maggephah* — the verb *atsar* means "to restrain, hold back, check." "Checked" conveys the sense of a force being arrested mid-course. "Stopped" (PNEUMA) implies full cessation too definitively. "Restrained" (EMET) is accurate but less natural in English.

---

### Verse 49 — "fourteen thousand and seven hundred" vs. "fourteen thousand seven hundred"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| **EMET** | "four-teen thousand and-seven hundred" |
| **LOGOS** | "fourteen thousand and seven hundred" |
| **PNEUMA** | "fourteen thousand seven hundred" |

**SOPHIA's ruling:** I adopt LOGOS. The Hebrew uses *ve-* (and) between the thousands and hundreds: *arba'ah asar elef u-sheva me'ot*. The conjunction should be preserved per binding conventions.

---

### Verse 50 — "was checked" vs. "was restrained" vs. "had been stopped"
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
